IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important applied problem—forecasting stock volatility from long-form 10-K disclosures—by proposing word-embedding-augmented IR weighting schemes and multimodal fusion with market features (Sections 1, 5 and 4). The approach reportedly "significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods" (claims comparing to Kogan et al., Wang et al., Tsai et al.) and uses a large SEC 2006–2015 dataset, making the results practically relevant. The methods (extending lexicon weights with Word2Vec similarities, MKL and stacking fusion) are likely to change how researchers combine lexical sentiment and embeddings for long documents and encourage further work on temporal drift and sector analysis (Fig.1 and discussion about noise from older years). While not paradigm-shifting, the contribution is a strong, broadly useful advance for financial NLP and volatility prediction.

CLARITY.justification: The manuscript has clear section headings (problem formulation, methodology, experiments) but the provided text is badly organized and contains extensive verbatim repetition and duplication (e.g. the paragraph beginning "In addition to using historic stock prices..." appears many times), which disrupts logical flow. Main contributions are stated only late and somewhat vaguely ("Our word embedding-based approach significantly outperforms..."), rather than clearly up front. Notation is sometimes adequate (Eq. 1 is defined) but cross-references and equation numbering are inconsistent and some implementation details are deferred without clear pointers (GARCH moved to supplementary). Figures and captions are poorly integrated (Figure 1 references (a)/(b) but the figure text and plots are hard to interpret here) and the text contains grammatical/formatting issues and stray footnotes, reducing readability.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper makes clear claims (e.g. that a word‑embedding IR weighting approach “significantly outperforms” prior work) and uses standard definitions (volatility formula Eq.1) and appropriate baselines (GARCH, Market, prior lexical methods). However, the provided text omits key experimental details and evidence: numerical results and statistical significance tests are not shown in the excerpt, GARCH implementation is deferred to supplementary material, and preprocessing choices (filtering volatilities beyond mean ±3σ) could bias outcomes but are not justified. Hyperparameter tuning, full train/test splitting or cross‑validation procedures are not described, and the exact weighting/expansion method is only referenced rather than fully specified — these gaps undermine certainty that the claims are fully supported.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper is squarely within applied NLP/IR at the intersection with finance: it develops sentiment analysis on long-form 10-K Risk Factors using word‑embedding–based IR weighting (sec. 1, Sentiment Analysis) and compares to prior lexicon/embedding work (Tsai & Wang, Wang et al.). The problem formulation (volatility labels y_k, Eq. 1–2) and evaluation (r2, MSE, baselines GARCH/Market and prior NLP baselines) match standard ML/NLP expectations (secs. 3–5). It is well targeted to an NLP/IR audience (document‑level sentiment, embedding-augmented weighting, fusion methods like MKL/stacking) though its primary novelty is applied rather than fundamental ML, so it may be more suitable for an applied NLP/IR track or interdisciplinary venue rather than a core ML theory conference.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper addresses a well-studied problem (volatility prediction from 10‑K/financial texts) and largely combines existing components: finance-specific lexicon (Loughran & McDonald), word2vec-based lexicon expansion (as in Tsai & Wang 2014), and embedding-aware IR weighting methods cited from Rekabsaz et al. (2016b). Fusion techniques (early fusion, stacking, MKL with mklaren) and GARCH/market baselines are standard; the contribution is an application and empirical evaluation of these known methods to long 10‑K Risk Factors rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic advance. Overall this is an incremental but practical composition of existing methods with moderate novelty.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments cover realistic baselines (GARCH, Market, Wang et al., Tsai et al.), multiple fusion strategies (early fusion, stacking, MKL/mklaren) and analyses over years and sectors (Fig.1 and discussion of concept drift), which gives reasonable empirical support. However, important reproducibility details are missing or deferred (GARCH implementation moved to supplementary, no word2vec or mklaren hyperparameters, unclear train/test splits and dataset size), ablation/sensitivity analyses of the proposed embedding-weighting variants are not clearly presented, and discussion of limitations is limited to a brief note about noisy/older data. These gaps justify a mid-level score (adequate but missing some important experiments and detail).

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares to appropriate, relevant baselines (GARCH, Market features, Kogan et al. 2009, Wang et al. 2013, Tsai & Wang 2014 and other recent 10‑K / sentiment works) and uses the same evaluation metric (r2) and similar learning settings (e.g. SVM with C=1) when re‑implementing baselines on their 2006–2015 SEC dataset (see Baselines and Evaluation Metrics). It also studies multiple fusion strategies and temporal drift (Figures and discussion), and cites the main competing approaches (Loughran & McDonald, Ding et al., Wang & Hua, etc.). However, the paper asserts it “significantly outperforms state‑of‑the‑art” without clearly reporting cases where prior methods might match or exceed performance, and some modern deep‑learning/event‑based baselines (discussed but not always re‑evaluated on the same document‑level dataset) are not thoroughly compared, so the comparisons are good but have minor omissions.
